name: Rumney Main Cliff
notes: Sport climbing mecca. West-facing schist dries quickly after rain.

name: Rumney Waimea
notes: South-facing, sun-soaked. Cooks in summer, magic in shoulder seasons.

name: Rumney Meadows
notes: Rumney's hardest lines cluster here. South-facing and sunny — ideal in cooler months.

name: Cathedral Ledge
notes: Classic granite trad and sport. Seeps after heavy rain, dries slow.

name: Whitehorse Ledge
notes: Across the valley from Cathedral. Long slab routes. SE aspect gets morning sun. Seeps linger in streaks.

name: Cannon Cliff
notes: Big alpine face. Cold, exposed, weather-sensitive. East-facing — morning sun.

name: Sundown Ledge
notes: Uncrowded NH granite sport and trad. South-facing and quick-drying.

name: Pawtuckaway
notes: Bouldering area. Forested, shaded boulders dry slower in humid weather.

name: Otter Cliffs
notes: Dramatic sea cliff above the Atlantic. Tidal spray affects base routes. Best at low tide, calm seas.

name: South Wall
notes: South-facing Acadia granite, dries quickly. Protected from north winds.

name: Canada Cliff
notes: Tallest cliff in Acadia. West-facing, afternoon sun. Great for sport and trad.

name: Chapel Ledge
notes: Vermont's best-developed sport crag. South-facing quartzite, quick-drying.

name: Smugglers' Notch
notes: Classic VT granite with bouldering and trad. Deep notch — shaded, stays damp longer.

name: Farley Ledge
notes: Massachusetts' best sport climbing. South-facing rhyolite dries very fast.

name: Crow Hill
notes: Central MA granite. Beginner-friendly with moderate classics. Dries well in south aspect.

name: Shawangunk Mountains (Trapps)
notes: The Gunks — legendary horizontal cracks and overhangs. South-facing, seeps after rain, needs 24–48h to dry.

name: Peterskill
notes: Gunks' quieter neighbor. Excellent face climbing on quartzite. Dries similarly to Trapps.

name: Breakneck Ridge
notes: Hudson Valley trad above Metro-North train stop. Schist can be lichenous. Best spring and fall.

name: Millbrook
notes: Gunks area outpost with classic overhanging trad. East-facing, dries moderate pace.

name: Palisades
notes: NYC metro classic above the Hudson. Basalt columns and face routes. Humid — allow extra drying time.

name: Ralph Stover State Park
notes: Best climbing near Philadelphia. Shale faces above Tohickon Creek. Avoid after heavy rain — rock is fragile when wet.

name: Carderock
notes: DC-area classic schist walls above the Potomac. Short but quality routes. Seeps after rain, dries in a day.

name: Great Falls (Virginia Side)
notes: Potomac River gorge schist. Technical face routes. Seepage common — east side dries faster.

name: Humpback Rocks
notes: Blue Ridge Parkway trad climbing on greenstone. West-facing. Some lichen on rock — best spring/fall.

name: New River Gorge
notes: Steep sandstone walls in America's newest National Park. North-facing — cooler temps. 1,400+ routes.

name: NRG – Kaymoor
notes: NRG's steepest sector. Long approach but worth it. North-facing — stays cool, dries moderately.

name: Summersville Lake
notes: Sandstone above a reservoir. Sport and trad. Water table affects base seepage — check after rain.

name: Cooper's Rock
notes: West Virginia sandstone near Morgantown. Mixed sun aspect. Quick-drying gritty sandstone.

name: Seneca Rocks
notes: Stunning quartzite fins rising 900 ft from the valley. Trad-focused. Exposed to wind, dries fast.

name: Linville Gorge
notes: NC's premier trad gorge. Steep quartzite walls. Wilderness permit may be required on weekends.

name: Table Rock
notes: Dramatic quartzite summit in the Gorge. East-facing — morning sun helps dry after rain.

name: Moore's Wall
notes: Hanging Rock SP quartzite. South-facing so dries fast. All-season climbing destination.

name: Stone Mountain
notes: Giant granite dome in NC. Smooth slabs and arches. South-facing dries fast.

name: Whiteside Mountain
notes: Tallest sheer cliff face in the eastern US. Peregrine falcon closures Feb–Aug on some routes. Damp — allow drying.

name: Pilot Mountain
notes: Striking NC quartzite knob. Short but quality trad and sport. Dries quickly in full sun.

name: Crowders Mountain
notes: Piedmont quartzite pinnacles near Charlotte. Good variety of trad routes. Dries quickly after rain.

name: Panthertown Valley
notes: NC's Yosemite. Multi-pitch slab granite. Elevation keeps temps cool. Allow 2+ days after rain.

name: Looking Glass Rock
notes: Classic Carolina granite dome. Slab and face climbing. Wet streaks linger — needs dry spell.

name: Rising Fawn / Rocktown
notes: Georgia's top bouldering destination. Sandstone blocks on Lookout Mountain plateau. Humid summers — autumn is best.

name: Tallulah Gorge
notes: Georgia quartzite in a dramatic river gorge. Permit required. Humidity can be high — check forecast.

name: Fox Mountain
notes: Granite face routes near Atlanta. South aspect for fast drying. One of GA's top sport crags.

name: Horse Pens 40
notes: Southern Appalachian sandstone bouldering on a private preserve. Sit starts on big moves. Humid in summer.

name: Sandrock
notes: Alabama sandstone sport climbing. Long walls with pockets and features. Dries fast in afternoon sun.

name: Foster Falls
notes: Gorgeous sandstone walls next to a waterfall. Tennessee's top sport climbing destination.

name: Obed – Lilly Bluff
notes: Obed Wild & Scenic River sandstone. South-facing dries fast after rain. Technical pocket pulling.

name: Sunset Wall
notes: Tennessee's best sport wall. West-facing sandstone — afternoon sun after rain accelerates drying.

name: Tennessee Wall
notes: Chattanooga's flagship crag. South-facing — fast drying and very popular. Year-round climbing.

name: Red River Gorge
notes: America's premier sport climbing destination. Overhanging pocketed sandstone. Dries quickly — rain drains fast off steep walls.

name: Muir Valley
notes: Private nature preserve within RRG. Free camping. Heavily overhanging walls shed rain quickly.

name: Natural Bridge
notes: RRG-adjacent sandstone with easier classics. North-facing — stays cool. Seeps longer after rain.

name: Devil's Lake
notes: Midwest's best climbing. Quartzite bluffs above a stunning lake. Dries relatively fast.

name: Taylors Falls
notes: Minnesota columnar basalt above the St. Croix River. Short routes but quality. Dries same day after light rain.

name: Elephant Rocks
notes: Ozark granite bouldering. Fun moderate terrain. Dries fast in Missouri heat.

name: Horseshoe Canyon Ranch
notes: Best sport climbing in the Midwest. Limestone-like sandstone. South-facing walls dry extremely fast after rain.

name: Sam's Throne
notes: Ozark sandstone bluffs with everything from 5.6 to 5.14. Camping on-site. Dries fast in dry weather.

name: Enchanted Rock
notes: Largest pink granite batholith in the US. Slab and dome climbing. Permit required. Best Oct–Apr.

name: Hueco Tanks
notes: World-class bouldering on pocketed syenite. Permit system required. Best Nov–Mar. Hot in summer.

name: Reimers Ranch
notes: Austin-area limestone sport climbing. Pockets and crimps. Humid summers — fall through spring preferred.

name: Pace Bend
notes: Central Texas limestone above Lake Travis. Short routes, popular with Austin climbers. Dries fast in Texas heat.

name: Palo Duro Canyon
notes: Grand Canyon of Texas. Colorful sandstone sport and trad. Desert climate — very fast drying.

name: Needles
notes: Black Hills granite spires and needles. Short classics on excellent rock. Dries fast in semi-arid climate.

name: Jackson Falls
notes: Illinois sandstone bouldering and short routes. West-facing walls dry same day in good conditions.

name: Clifty Falls
notes: Indiana sandstone gorge. Mossy and damp — seeps after rain. Best in late spring and fall.

name: Eldorado Canyon
notes: Colorado's premier trad area. South Boulder Creek canyon. Seeps after rain, moderate drying time.

name: Rifle Mountain Park
notes: World-class steep limestone sport climbing. Overhangs shed water well — often climbable same day as rain.

name: Shelf Road
notes: Arkansas River Valley limestone. South-facing — excellent winter crag and fast-drying after rain.

name: Boulder Canyon
notes: Granite canyon 10 min from Boulder. Sport and trad. Shaded canyon dries slower — check aspect.

name: Clear Creek Canyon
notes: Denver's closest sport climbing. Granite and gneiss. North-facing sections stay damp — pick south walls after rain.

name: Lumpy Ridge
notes: Rocky Mountain trad classic. Granite domes above Estes Park. Afternoon thunderstorms July–Aug.

name: Penitente Canyon
notes: Southern CO volcanic rhyolite. South-facing and very sunny — fast drying. Unique featured rock.

name: Black Canyon of the Gunnison
notes: America's most serious climbing. Multi-pitch trad on remote walls. Technical approach — dry conditions essential.

name: Ten Sleep Canyon
notes: World-class limestone sport climbing in Wyoming. Caves and overhangs stay dry. Sunny and arid — dries fast.

name: Sinks Canyon
notes: Lander area limestone and granite. South-facing walls dry same day. Short drive from town.

name: Wild Iris
notes: High-quality limestone sport climbing on Wyoming plateau. South-facing — fast drying in dry climate.

name: Vedauwoo
notes: Erratic granite boulders with wild off-width and wide cracks. Windy and arid. Dries very quickly.

name: City of Rocks
notes: Ancient granite spires in southern Idaho. Dry climate dries fast. High desert altitude — cool evenings year-round.

name: Dierkes Lake
notes: Idaho basalt bouldering and short sport routes above the Snake River canyon. Dries fast in arid high desert.

name: Square Butte
notes: Remote Montana basalt tower. Limited development but excellent rock quality. Arid — dries quickly.

name: Maple Canyon
notes: Unique cobblestone conglomerate. Pockets everywhere. Shaded canyon, cool in summer. Seeps minimally.

name: American Fork Canyon
notes: World-class limestone 30 min from Salt Lake City. Steep pockets and edges. Seeps after rain — needs 24h.

name: Little Cottonwood Canyon
notes: World-class granite in the Wasatch. South-facing walls dry fast. Snowmelt affects canyon access April–May.

name: Big Cottonwood Canyon
notes: Easy access from SLC. Classic moderate granite. South-facing dries quickly.

name: Indian Creek
notes: The crack climbing capital of the world. Wingate sandstone splitters. Desert climate — very fast drying.

name: Joe's Valley
notes: World-class bouldering destination. High-quality sandstone. Dries fast in Utah's arid climate. Best fall and spring.

name: Zion National Park
notes: Big wall sandstone in a National Park. Permit-heavy logistics. Desert drying — avoid after flash flood risk.

name: St. George Crags
notes: Desert climbing near Zion. Year-round destination. South aspect — hot in summer, ideal Oct–Apr.

name: Logan Canyon
notes: Utah limestone in a scenic canyon. Mixed aspects — south-facing walls dry faster.

name: Red Rock Canyon
notes: Stunning red sandstone 20 min from Las Vegas. Desert-dry conditions, excellent friction. Best Oct–May.

name: Black Velvet Canyon
notes: Red Rock's big wall sector. Shaded north-facing canyon — cooler but seeps longer after rain.

name: Mount Lemmon
notes: Sky island granite above Tucson. High elevation keeps temps cool year-round. Monsoon season July–Sept.

name: Granite Mountain
notes: Central AZ granite above Prescott. South-facing — fast drying. Elevated enough to avoid summer heat.

name: Queen Creek Canyon
notes: Phoenix-area sport and trad climbing. Quartzite walls dry very fast in desert climate. Hot summers, ideal winters.

name: Cochise Stronghold
notes: Historic Apache granite in SE Arizona. Secluded, world-class trad climbing. Monsoon July–Sept — avoid or check conditions.

name: Sedona
notes: Stunning red rock sandstone. Fragile desert rock — avoid when wet. Dries very fast in desert sun.

name: Jack's Canyon
notes: Arizona's hidden limestone gem. Long, sustained sport routes. Desert-arid, dries instantly.

name: Cochiti Mesa
notes: New Mexico volcanic crags near Santa Fe. South-facing and arid — dries same day. Unique welded tuff climbing.

name: Enchanted Tower
notes: Remote NM rhyolite sport climbing. South-facing — fast drying in high desert.

name: Yosemite Valley
notes: World-famous big wall and crack climbing. Spring seepage on big walls. Best: April-June, Sept-Oct.

name: Tuolumne Meadows
notes: High Sierra slabs and domes. Short season (July–Sept). Afternoon thunderstorms common in summer.

name: Bishop
notes: World capital of bouldering. Volcanic and granite highballs. Desert climate — fast drying year-round.

name: Lover's Leap
notes: Sierra Nevada granite slab and crack climbing. South-facing — great drying after afternoon showers.

name: Donner Summit
notes: I-80 corridor granite. South-facing slabs dry fast. Snow possible until June.

name: Joshua Tree
notes: Desert trad and face climbing paradise. 8,000+ routes. Best Oct–May. Hot in summer.

name: Tahquitz Rock
notes: Historic Southern California granite. Birthplace of the Yosemite Decimal System. Cool temps year-round due to elevation.

name: Suicide Rock
notes: Idyllwild's second great summit. West-facing — afternoon sun helps drying. Less popular than Tahquitz.

name: Mount Woodson
notes: San Diego area bouldering. Excellent granite. Dry SoCal climate — dries very fast.

name: Pinnacles National Park
notes: Volcanic spires and columns. Falcon closures Feb–July on some walls. Dries fast in Central Coast heat.

name: Castle Rock
notes: Bay Area sandstone bouldering and short routes. Tafoni texture. Humid — dries slower than desert crags.

name: Malibu Creek
notes: LA-area conglomerate bouldering. South-facing — fast drying after coastal fog clears.

name: Smith Rock
notes: Birthplace of American sport climbing. Rhyolite tuff dries fast. Wind can be strong. Best Apr–Oct.

name: Smith Rock – Marsupial Crags
notes: Smith Rock's south-facing sector — maximum sun exposure. Often climbable same day as rain.

name: Broughton Bluff
notes: Portland's backyard basalt crag. South-facing — driest in Oregon by local standards. Year-round accessible.

name: Columbia River Gorge
notes: Basalt columns and faces in the gorge. Windy microclimate. Allow 24–48h drying after Pacific rain events.

name: Table Rock
notes: Willamette Valley basalt. South-facing — one of Oregon's better-drying crags.

name: Index Town Walls
notes: Steep, burly crack climbing in the Pacific Northwest. Wet climate — needs extended dry weather to be climbable.

name: Index Upper Town Wall
notes: Harder lines above the Lower Wall. South-facing but wet climate. Needs 3–5 dry days to be in condition.

name: Frenchman Coulee
notes: East of the Cascades — driest WA climbing. Columnar basalt. Dries very fast. Strong desert winds possible.

name: Beacon Rock
notes: Volcanic plug on the Columbia. Multi-pitch basalt. Wet side of Cascades — allow 2–3 days after rain.

name: Leavenworth – Castle Rock
notes: Granite crags in a Bavarian-themed town. East of the Cascades — drier than the coast. Spring and fall best.

name: Peshastin Pinnacles
notes: Unique WA sandstone spires. South-facing dry side — dries fast for Pacific Northwest. Fragile rock when wet.

name: Tieton
notes: Central WA basalt in the Yakima River canyon. Rain shadow zone — dries significantly faster than the coast.

name: Mount Erie
notes: Anacortes conglomerate. Car-top access. Wet Puget Sound climate — needs extended dry period.

name: Exit 38
notes: Seattle's closest granite sport climbing. South-facing but still WA — allow 2 days after rain.
